user_query: During a local league game, 2.5 level, an opponent called a foot fault. Is that allowed?
expected_rule_reference: The Code §24, with local context from app hierarchy
right_answer: In an unofficiated local league match, an opponent may call a foot fault only after all reasonable efforts, such as warning the server and attempting to get an official, have failed, and only when the foot fault is so flagrant that it is clearly perceptible from the receiver’s side.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: The receiver or the receiver’s partner may call foot faults only after all reasonable efforts, such as warning the server and attempting to get an official to the court, have failed and the foot fault is so flagrant as to be clearly perceptible from the receiver’s side.

must_not_include: opponents can freely call any suspected foot fault

user_query: Server served before the receiver was ready. Do they get two serves again?
right_answer: If the serve is delivered when the receiver is not ready, that is a service let. That particular service does not count, the server serves again, and a service let does not cancel a previous fault. So if this happened on a second serve, the server serves the second serve again, not automatically a first serve.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: A service let occurs when the ball is served when the receiver is not ready. In the case of a service let, that particular service shall not count, and the server shall serve again, but a service let does not cancel a previous fault.

must_include: service let OR serve does not count as a fault
previous fault is not cancelled OR still on second serve if it was a second serve

user_query: If the ball hits a player before bouncing, who wins the point?
right_answer: The player who is hit loses the point, because a player loses the point if the ball in play touches the player or anything the player is wearing or carrying, except the racket.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: The point is lost if the ball in play touches the player or anything that the player is wearing or carrying, except the racket.

must_include: player hit by ball loses point

user_query: A player is 12 minutes late to a regular local league match in PNW. What penalty applies?
right_answer: For a regular local league match in PNW, a player who is 12 minutes late is defaulted. The PNW lateness table says 10:01 or more minutes late is a default.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: When a player is late... the lateness penalty is as follows: 5 minutes or less late: loss of service toss plus one game; 5:01–10 minutes late: loss of service toss plus 2 games; 10:01 or more minutes late: Default.

user_query: A player is 12 minutes late to a local league playoff match in PNW. What penalty applies?
right_answer: For a PNW local league playoff match, a player who is 12 minutes late loses the service toss plus 3 games. The playoff/weekend lateness table uses 10:01–15 minutes late as loss of service toss plus 3 games, with default only at 15 or more minutes late.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Single Weekend Leagues and Local League Playoff matches will be as follows... 5 minutes or less late: loss of service toss plus one game; 5:01–10 minutes late: loss of service toss plus 2 games; 10:01–15 minutes late: loss of service toss plus 3 games; 15 or more minutes late: Default.

must_include: loss of service toss plus 3 games

user_query: A player was late to the match. What happens?
right_answer: It depends on the match context in PNW. For a regular local league match, 10:01 or more minutes late is a default. For single weekend leagues and local league playoff matches, 10:01–15 minutes late is loss of service toss plus 3 games and default starts at 15 or more minutes late. The answer should present those contexts separately rather than blending them.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Regular local league lateness: 10:01 or more minutes late: Default.
Single Weekend Leagues and Local League Playoff matches... 10:01–15 minutes late: loss of service toss plus 3 games; 15 or more minutes late: Default.

must_include: regular local league vs playoff/weekend split

user_query: In a match with a chair umpire, can players still resolve line calls using The Code?
expected_rule_reference: Role of Court Officials, Appendix VII
expected_citation_string: Friend at Court, Role of Court Officials, Appendix VII
right_answer: No. In a match with a chair umpire, the chair umpire is the final authority on questions of fact during the match. Players may call the referee only if they disagree with the chair umpire’s interpretation of tennis law, so players do not resolve those calls under The Code as if the match were unofficiated.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: In matches where a chair umpire is assigned, the chair umpire is the final authority on all questions of fact during the match. The players have the right to call the referee to court if they disagree with a chair umpire’s interpretation of tennis law.

must_include: chair umpire is final authority on questions of fact
must_not_include: players still resolve line calls under The Code the same way

user_query: If a player creates a hindrance but there is a roving official, who decides the outcome?
expected_rule_reference: Role of Court Officials, Appendix VII
expected_citation_string: Friend at Court, Role of Court Officials, Appendix VII
right_answer: If an official is present and acting in that match, the official decides the outcome on questions of fact within the official’s authority. In officiated play, the match is not governed as if players are self-officiating under The Code.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: In matches where a chair umpire is assigned, the chair umpire is the final authority on all questions of fact during the match.

user_query: What rule says I have to call balls on my side only?
right_answer: The Code says a player calls all shots landing on, or aimed at, the player’s side of the net.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Player makes calls on own side of net. A player calls all shots landing on, or aimed at, the player's side of the net.

must_include: player makes calls on own side of net

user_query: I served and the returner missed it, then said they weren’t ready. They didn’t say anything before I served. Do they get the point replayed?
right_answer: Not automatically. Under The Code, if a player attempts to return a serve, even a quick serve, the receiver is presumed to be ready. So if the returner tried to play the serve and only objected after missing it, the default answer is that they do not get the point replayed on a bare claim of not being ready.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: If a player attempts to return a serve (even if it is a 'quick' serve), then the receiver is presumed to be ready.

must_include: attempt to return means presumed ready
must_not_include: receiver always gets replayed point afterward

user_query: I accidentally hit the ball twice in one swing. Opponent says it’s illegal. Is it?
right_answer: An unintentional double hit is not treated as hindrance, and Rule 24(f) only makes a player lose the point for deliberately touching the ball with the racket more than once. So an accidental double contact in one swing is not automatically illegal on that basis.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: The point is lost if the player deliberately touches the ball with the racket more than once.
Case 1: Is an unintentional double hit a hindrance? Decision: No. See also Rule 24(f).

must_include: unintentional double hit is not hindrance
must_not_include: any double contact loses the point

user_query: My captain says opponents are never allowed to call foot faults. Is that true?
right_answer: No. That is too absolute. In an unofficiated match, the receiver or the receiver’s partner may call foot faults, but only after all reasonable efforts such as warning the server and attempting to get an official have failed, and only when the foot fault is flagrant and clearly perceptible from the receiver’s side.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: The receiver or the receiver's partner may call foot faults only after all reasonable efforts... have failed and the foot fault is so flagrant as to be clearly perceptible from the receiver's side.

user_query: Any time a point is replayed, you always get two serves again, right?
right_answer: No. Under ITF Rule 23, when a let is called the whole point is replayed except when a service let is called on a second service. The Code also says that when there is a delay between first and second serves, the server gets one serve if the server caused the delay and two serves if the receiver caused it or there was outside interference.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: In all cases when a let is called, except when a service let is called on a second service, the whole point shall be replayed.

When there is a delay between the first and second serves: the server gets one serve if the server was the cause of the delay; the server gets two serves if the delay was caused by the receiver or if there was outside interference.

must_not_include: all replays reset to first serve

user_query: My opponent is making terrible line calls and won’t change them. What am I allowed to do?
right_answer: You are not allowed to start making calls on your opponent’s side. The Code says each player calls shots landing on that player's side, opponents get the benefit of the doubt, and any ball that cannot be called out is good.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: A player calls all shots landing on, or aimed at, the player's side of the net.
Any doubt must be resolved in favor of an opponent.
Any ball that cannot be called out is considered to be good.

must_include: ball not clearly out is good
must_not_include: retaliate with your own line calls

user_query: I think my opponent is cheating on line calls. Can I start calling their shots on my side?
right_answer: No. The Code requires a player to make calls only on that player's own side, gives the opponent the benefit of the doubt, and treats any ball that cannot be called out as good.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: A player calls all shots landing on, or aimed at, the player's side of the net.
Any ball that cannot be called out is considered to be good.

user_query: We’re in a dispute and can’t agree on what happened. Who decides in a normal league match?
expected_rule_reference: The Code §§5-8; Friend at Court Appendix VII for officiated contrast
expected_citation_string: The Code §§5-8; Friend at Court Appendix VII
right_answer: In a normal unofficiated league match, the players make the calls on their own side and apply the unofficiated principles in The Code. If there is a chair umpire, the chair umpire becomes the final authority on questions of fact.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: A player calls all shots landing on, or aimed at, the player's side of the net.
In matches where a chair umpire is assigned, the chair umpire is the final authority on all questions of fact during the match.

must_include: unofficiated players decide their own side
chair umpire contrast if officiated play is mentioned

user_query: Opponent shouted after I hit the ball but before it landed. Does that count as hindrance?
right_answer: Potentially yes. Under Rule 26, if a player is hindered by a deliberate act of the opponent, the hindered player wins the point. If the hindrance is caused by an unintentional act of the opponent, the point is replayed.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: If a player is hindered in playing the point by a deliberate act of the opponent(s), the player shall win the point. However, the point shall be replayed if a player is hindered by either an unintentional act of the opponent(s), or something outside the player's own control.

must_not_include: all talking is automatic loss of point

user_query: There was a hindrance during my second serve. Do I get a first or second serve?
expected_rule_reference: Rule 23 and Rule 26; supplemental The Code §30
right_answer: It depends on the type of interruption. If it is a service let because the receiver was not ready, that particular service does not count and a previous fault is not canceled, so a second serve stays a second serve. If the point is replayed because of unintentional hindrance or outside interference after play has started, Rule 23 says the whole point is replayed except for a service let on second serve, and The Code says outside interference between first and second serves gives the server two serves.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: In all cases when a let is called, except when a service let is called on a second service, the whole point shall be replayed.
If a player is hindered by an unintentional act of the opponent(s), the point shall be replayed.
When the server’s second service motion is interrupted by a ball coming onto the court, the server is entitled to two serves.

must_include: service let on second serve exception or outside-interference distinction

user_query: USTA rules say X, but our local league does Y. Which one actually applies?
expected_rule_reference: App hierarchy + PNW League Regulations as highest-priority local source
right_answer: In a PNW local league match, the local PNW League Regulations control when they directly address the issue. Your app’s hierarchy puts PNW League Regulations above national USTA League Regulations, The Code, Friend at Court, and the ITF Rules.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: PNW League Regulations are the highest-priority source, followed by USTA League Regulations (National), The Code, Friend at Court, and ITF Rules of Tennis.

user_query: Friend at Court says one thing but The Code says something else. Which one should I follow?
right_answer: Under your app’s hierarchy, The Code outranks Friend at Court for unofficiated-match questions unless a higher-priority local or national league rule directly controls the issue.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: The Code is priority 3 and Friend at Court is priority 4.

must_include: The Code outranks Friend at Court

user_query: Isn’t there a rule that says you lose a point if your hat falls off?
expected_rule_reference: No such rule identified in sourced materials used here
right_answer: A correct answer should not invent such a rule. Based on the sourced materials used here, there is no identified rule saying a player automatically loses the point merely because a hat falls off.

evidence.source: Sourced rules set used for this gold subset
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: No direct rule identified for automatic point loss solely because a hat falls off.

user_query: If the ball hits the line but spins out, it’s out, right?
right_answer: No. If a ball touches a line, it is regarded as touching the court bounded by that line. What it does after the bounce does not change that.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: If a ball touches a line, it is regarded as touching the court bounded by that line.

user_query: If someone is racist to me during a tennis match, what should happen?
expected_rule_reference: Rule 26 (hindrance); USTA officiating guidance (default for flagrant conduct); USTA League Suspension Point System
expected_citation_string: ITF Rule 26; Friend at Court officiating guidance; USTA League Regulations
right_answer: Racist abuse is not allowed. If it occurs during a point and it hinders the opponent, Rule 26 says the hindered player wins the point for a deliberate hindrance. In officiated USTA play, racial slurs can justify an immediate default as flagrant unsportsmanlike conduct. In league play, the conduct can also be reported through the grievance process and may lead to suspension-point penalties or other discipline.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: If a player is hindered in playing the point by a deliberate act of the opponent(s), the player shall win the point.
An Official may immediately default a player for a single flagrant unsportsmanlike act, including using racial slurs.
Any player participating in a USTA League match consents to being penalized for any unsportsmanlike conduct.

must_include: possible default for flagrant unsportsmanlike conduct

user_query: What about if they offer a substitute for a late player?
expected_rule_reference: USTA League Regulations - Lineup and Substitution Rules
right_answer: No, a substitute is generally not allowed after lineups have been exchanged. Substitutions must follow league regulations and typically must occur before lineup exchange. If a player is late and no valid substitution is permitted under the rules, the position is subject to default.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Lineups are exchanged prior to the match and become official at that time. Player substitutions are governed by league regulations and are generally not permitted after lineup exchange.
evidence.citation: USTA League Regulations - Lineup Exchange Section

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Local league rules define when substitutions are allowed; typically substitutions must be made before lineup exchange or within specific local allowances.

must_include: substitution not allowed after lineup exchange OR lineup is locked
possible default if no valid player
must_not_include: players can freely substitute at any time
opponent agreement overrides league rules automatically

user_query: What if both captains agree to allow a substitute for a late player?
expected_rule_reference: USTA League Regulations - Lineup and Substitution Rules
right_answer: No, even if both captains agree, substitutions must follow league regulations. Once lineups are exchanged, they are official and cannot be changed unless the specific league rules explicitly allow it. Captain agreement does not override league rules, and an invalid substitution could result in a default or disqualification.

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Lineups become official upon exchange and must comply with league regulations. Matches must be played in accordance with the rules, and captains cannot override these rules by agreement.
evidence.citation: USTA League Regulations - Lineup Exchange Section

evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: Agreements between players or captains that are contrary to the Rules of Tennis or applicable regulations are not valid.

must_include: invalid substitution may lead to default or disqualification
must_not_include: agreement between captains makes it automatically valid
rules can be ignored if both teams agree
